segments.s528-g89: سوی پارس is a prepositional phrase meaning “toward Pars” placed before the verb; آمد is the past tense verb “came.” ز introduces the place of origin (poetic for از, “from”) and نامجوی is a proper name (the subject/agent). Word order is typical of classical Persian poetry (direction — verb — origin — name).
segments.s528-g90: برآسوده is an adjectival/predicative form meaning “at ease/relieved” describing the person; از marks the source ‘from’ (here: از رزم = “from battle”) and وز here functions idiomatically as a poetic connector meaning roughly “and (from)” or “and also from” (a fused و + ز construction). گفت‌وگوی is the noun “conversation/dispute” governed by از/وز, so the phrase means “relieved from war and from dispute.”
segments.s528-g91: یکی is the indefinite “a certain,” شارستان is the noun “town/city” and کرد is the past tense verb “made” (he caused/constructed). پر qualifies the following nouns as “full of,” so پر کاخ و باغ = “full of palaces and gardens.” The implied subject (he/one) is omitted, common in Persian.
segments.s528-g92: بدو is the dative/enclitic-like particle meaning “to it/unto him” (here: “to/for the town”), اندرون means “within/inside,” and the following nouns are a coordinated list (چشمه و دشت و راغ) listing features contained within the town. The sequence enumerates internal elements (springs, plains, and راغ).
segments.s528-g93: که introduces a relative clause (“which/that/who”), اکنون is “now,” and گرانمایه is an honorific adjective “esteemed/venerable.” دهقان here refers to the dihqān (landed gentry/important rural magnate) and پیر means “old” or “elder,” so the phrase describes the man now as “the venerable old dehqan.”
segments.s528-g94: همی is an older/perfective-imperfective adverbial marker often meaning “habitually/still”; خواندش is خواند + -ش (past form with the clitic object “him”) and here means “called him” (in classical usage خواندن can mean ‘to name/call’). خوره is an epithet attached to اردشیر (rendered as a sobriquet), so the clause reads “they called him ‘Khore Ardashir.’”
segments.s528-g95: یکی is the indefinite “a certain,” چشمه is “spring,” and بی‌کران is the adjective “boundless.” اندرروی (a poetic variant of اندرون/اندروی) means “inwardly / in its interior.” بد appears here in an adverbial or emphatic/archaic position modifying the phrase, but its ordinary senses (bad/ill) do not fit this context and the function is idiomatic/poetic.
segments.s528-g96: فراوان is an adverb/adjective “abundantly/plentiful,” ازو = از + او “from it,” رود here is a noun meaning “river/torrent” and بگشاد is an older past form (ب + گشاد/گشود) meaning “opened” or “burst forth.” The coordinating و links رود بگشاد and جوی (جوی = “stream”), so the sense is that abundantly a river burst forth from it and a stream (also flowed).
segments.s528-g25: ز بس introduces a cause/quantity: ز (poetic 'from'/'because of') + بس ('so much'/'many') — together 'from so many'. The main clause is elliptical: کشته شد is passive/impersonal ('were killed' / 'were slain') and روی هامون ('on the surface/plain of Hamun') is the locative phrase; چو کوه is a simile ('like a mountain') modifying the result. Word order is poetic (verb before its locative complement and the simile), and شد here functions as 'became/was' resulting from the mass of slain.
segments.s528-g26: بشد is the past/resultative 'became/it came to be'. خسته is a predicative adjective 'weary/tired'. از زندگانی sets the cause or domain ('of life' / 'from life'), and ستوه is a second adjective meaning 'sick of' or 'wearied with' that reinforces خسته. The clause therefore says the (implicit) person became exhausted and sick of living; the two adjectives form a stacked predicate after the verb.
segments.s528-g27: سرانجام is an adverb meaning 'finally' or 'in the end'. ابری here is a noun 'a cloud' (poetic adjective-to-noun use), and برآمد is the past 'arose/appeared'. سیاه follows to qualify the cloud ('dark/gloomy'); the phrase literally reads 'finally a cloud arose, dark.'
segments.s528-g28: بشد is again 'it became/was formed'. کوشش و رزم را دستگاه is an object phrase marked by را: 'the apparatus/system (دستگاه) of effort and battle' — کوشش و رزم form a compound genitive-like description of دستگاه (the را marks the whole phrase as definite/affected). The clause means the war‑machinery or system of effort was established.
segments.s528-g29: یکی before a noun here is indefinite ('a certain' / 'one') and introduces باد 'a wind'. برخاست is the past 'rose/started up' and از انجمن gives the source ('from the assembly/gathering'). The order is typical of poetry (determiner + noun + verb + source).
segments.s528-g30: دل جنگیان is a genitival phrase 'the hearts of the warriors' (the ezāfe is omitted in verse). گشت is 'became' or 'turned into'. زان ('from that' / 'therefore') links this clause to the preceding event (the wind), and پرشکن is a predicate adjective meaning something like 'broken/terrified' or 'hard‑shattering' — together 'the warriors' hearts became, because of that, broken/terrified.'
segments.s528-g31: بتوفید is a poetic past form (ب‑ prefix + توفید) meaning 'spat' or 'spewed forth' and is used vividly; the verb precedes its subject (کوه) by poetic inversion. و connects to the next clause: بلرزید دشت is 'the plain trembled' (past tense verb + noun), forming two linked pictorial actions.
segments.s528-g32: خروشش is the noun 'its roar' (خروش + possessive suffix). همی is a continuative/adverbial particle here ('still/continually'). از هوا is a source/instrumental phrase 'through/from the air', and برگذشت is the past 'passed by/traversed'. The clause states the roar continued to pass through the air.
segments.s528-g105: This is a simple past-clause. به جایی یکی = a prepositional phrase 'to/at a certain place' (به + جایی + یکی, with یکی specifying 'a certain'), ژرف دریا = 'deep sea' with the adjective ژرف placed before the noun (a poetic word order), and بدید is the archaic/poetic past 3rd-singular of دیدن 'to see'. The whole phrase means 'he saw a deep sea at a certain place.'
segments.s528-g106: همی is a narrative/progressive particle (common in epic style) marking the ongoing or immediate action. کوه is the subject 'the mountain'; بایست here is an intransitive past meaning 'stood/rose up' (from ایستادن); پیشش is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'before him/in front of him' (پیش + pronoun -ش), and برید in this context functions as a verbal motion 'came upon/approached' (poetic usage derived from بر‑). The clause reads 'then a mountain stood up and advanced before him.'
segments.s528-g107: ببردند is a past 3rd‑plural form (they took/carried/tore away). میتین is an uncommon/rare lexical item (here left untranslated as a rare form or name linked to the following apposition), and 'و مردان کار' is a nominative apposition 'and the men of work/deed' (i.e. workers or active men). Grammatically the verbs govern an omitted object (what they carried) and the nouns name the agents.
segments.s528-g108: وزان = 'from that' (و + زِ + آن/shortened) introducing origin; کوه is the locative/source 'mountain'; ببرید here is again past (they cut/took out/made from) with an implicit plural subject carried over from the previous clause; صد جویبار is a numeral phrase 'a hundred streams' (صد before the counted noun). The clause means 'and from that mountain they cut/made a hundred streams.'
segments.s528-g109: همی again is the narrative particle. راند is past 3rd‑singular of راندن 'to drive/expel/urge on' and takes a from…to phrase: از کوه تا شهر = 'from the mountain to the city' (تا here = 'as far as/to'). گور in this construction reads as 'grave' (or a place named Gūr) completing the place-expression 'شهر گور' ('the city of graves' / 'Gur city'). The phrase expresses movement/expulsion over that extent.
segments.s528-g110: شد is the past/auxiliary 'became' or 'was made'. آن شارستان = 'that town/city' (آن as demonstrative). پر is the adjective 'full (of)', followed by سرای (here used as 'houses/dwellings' in the phrase) and و ستور 'and cattle/beasts' — a stock formula meaning the place became full of dwellings and livestock. The clause therefore reads 'that town became full of houses and cattle.'
segments.s528-g57: The clause begins with the conditional particle اگر (if). تا here functions as an intensifying/as‑far‑as marker ('even / as far as'), not a temporal 'until'. ستاره is the subject, and برآرد is a classical 3rd‑person singular present/subjunctive form of برآوردن used here with a future or potential sense ('raise' / 'bring up' / 'attain'); بلند follows as an adverbial/adjectival complement 'high, lofty'.
segments.s528-g58: سپارد is a simple present/gnomic form of سپردن used with a future/resultive force here ('will consign / will deliver'). هم is an adverbial emphasizer ('even/also'), and آخر is a temporal/adverbial noun 'at last / finally'. به خاک is a prepositional phrase 'to the earth', with نژند post‑modifying خاک as an adjective meaning 'base / lowly / worthless'.
segments.s528-g59: دو فرزند او is the noun phrase 'his two children' (noun + possessor). هم after the pronoun marks 'also'. گرفتار شد is a light‑verb/periphrastic expression: گرفتار (captured/afflicted) + شد ('became/was'), producing the past/result 'were taken / were trapped'.
segments.s528-g60: برو here is a connective/temporal particle 'then / thereupon' (not a literal imperative 'go'), introducing the next result. تخمه آرشی is a genitival phrase 'the seed/offspring (تخمه) of Arshi/Arash' (آرشی = of Arash; a proper or attributive form), and خوار شد is a past passive/result phrase 'became despised / became lowly' (خوار = despised, شد = became).
segments.s528-g61: آن هر دو را is the definite object 'those two'. پای کرده is a participial construction (پای + کرده) meaning 'having made/fastened the feet' or idiomatically 'bound (by the feet)'; به بند is a prepositional phrase 'in fetters / in bonds'. The initial مر is an archaic/poetic particle/clitic (appearing in older usage) that here introduces or colors the action—its literal sense 'me / to me' is not active; in context it functions like 'thereupon' or simply a connective.
segments.s528-g62: The locative phrase به زندان ('to/into prison') comes before the verb for emphatic word order. فرستاد is simple past 'sent', and شاه بلند follows the verb as the subject with an epithet (بلند = 'lofty' / 'exalted'), a typical classical Persian V‑S word order for emphasis.
segments.s528-g63: دو بدمهر is the subject 'two ill‑hearted / ill‑disposed (people)'. از رزم is a prepositional phrase 'from battle', placed before the verb for emphasis. بگریختند is the 3rd‑person plural past form 'they fled / escaped' (the prefix ب‑ here is an older verbal form, common in classical texts).
segments.s528-g64: به دام بلا is a noun phrase 'into the trap/snare of calamity' (به + دام + بلا). در نیاویختند is a negative past verb: نیاویختند (from آویختن/آویختن meaning 'to cling/fall into') with the negative prefix 'ن‑', so 'they did not fall/cling into' — idiomatically 'they did not fall into the snare'.
segments.s528-g65: برفتند is a past tense, 3rd-person plural verb ('they departed/went'). گریان is a participial/adverbial adjective ('weeping') describing the manner and follows the verb; به هندوستان is a prepositional phrase of direction ('to India') with به before the place-name.
segments.s528-g66: This is a conditional/purpose clause: سزدِگر (compound of سزد 'be fitting' + گر 'if') expresses 'if it is fitting', followed by کنی, the 2nd-person present/subjunctive/imperative sense 'you make/do' (here 'make a tale'). زین is an older contracted form meaning 'from this' (not listed correctly in the provided senses), سخن is 'words/speech', and داستان is the noun 'tale/story' that is the object of کنی.
segments.s528-g67: همه here means 'the whole/entire'; رزمگه is 'battlefield/arena'. پر is the adjective 'full (of)' placed before its noun complement; ستام (here 'praise') is the content that fills the battlefield. The coordinating و links this phrase to the following noun کمر ('waist/girdle'), which begins the next coordinated clause.
segments.s528-g68: پر از is the fixed pattern 'full of'; از marks the source/contents ('of/with'), followed by a series of nouns joined by و: آلت ('arms/equipment'), لشکر ('host/troops'), سیم ('silver' — implied here though not separately listed), and زر ('gold'). The nouns are the items filling the girdles/waists.
segments.s528-g69: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past tense 'he commanded/said'. تا introduces a subordinate clause meaning 'so that' or 'that', and گرد کردند is a verbal phrase 'they gathered/assembled' (گرد + کردن). شاه appears after the clause by poetic inversion and refers back to the agent/title (the king) associated with بفرمود.
segments.s528-g70: ببخشید here means 'he bestowed/gave' (not 'forgave'), زان پس is a temporal phrase 'after that/thereafter' (زان = 'from that', پس = 'after/then'), and همه بر سپاه means 'all (things) upon/to the army' with بر marking the recipient/target ('to/upon the army').
segments.s528-g71: برفت here functions as a past verb 'he departed/withdrew' (the provided lemma senses refer to 'snow' and do not match this usage). از میان is the prepositional compound 'from among', بزرگان is the plural 'the great/important ones', and سباک is an epithet 'smiths/metal-workers' (in apposition), giving 'from among the great smiths.'
segments.s528-g72: تن اردوان را is a definite object phrase ('Ardavan's body') with را marking the definite object; ز خون is an ablative/genitive phrase 'of/from the blood' describing what is removed; کرد پاک is the verbal phrase 'made (it) clean' — i.e. 'cleansed/wiped (the body) of blood.' Word order places the object before the verb, typical in Persian.
segments.s528-g49: This is an inverted word order common in Persian poetry: the indirect object phrase 'به دژخیم' ('to the torturer') comes first, followed by the verb 'فرمود' (past tense 'commanded/said') and then the subject 'شاه اردشیر' ('King Ardashir'). 'به' here marks the recipient of the utterance/command.
segments.s528-g50: 'که' introduces reported content ('that' / 'that he said: ...'). The noun phrase 'رو دشمن پادشا را' is the definite object (marked by 'را') and contains the modifier order 'دشمن پادشا' = 'the king's enemy' or 'the enemy king'. 'رو' here is a short poetic noun meaning 'face/person' used emphatically as part of the object, and 'بگیر' is an imperative ('take/seize!') addressed to the torturer.
segments.s528-g51: Imperative construction: the instrument 'به خنجر' ('with/by a dagger') comes first, then the affected part 'میانش' ('his middle') and the result phrase 'به دو نیم' ('into two halves'), followed by the light imperative 'کن' ('do/make'). The first 'به' marks the instrument ('by/with') and the second 'به' marks the resultant division ('into').
segments.s528-g52: Another imperative clause: 'دل بدسگالان' ('the hearts of the ill-omened') is the subject/possessed phrase, 'پر از بیم' = 'full of fear' (the structure 'پر از X' = 'full of X'), and the final 'کن' is the imperative 'make/fill'. 'بدسگالان' is a plural adjective/noun meaning those of bad omen or ill‑fate.
segments.s528-g53: 'بیامد' is simple past 'he came' with the subject 'دژآگاه' (the fortress-warden) following it — a common poetic order. The conjunction 'و' links the next verb phrase 'فرمان گزید'; here 'گزید' (literally 'chose') is used idiomatically with 'فرمان' to mean he accepted/obeyed or carried out the command.
segments.s528-g54: 'شد' is the past auxiliary 'became/was' and introduces the result clause: 'آن نامدار' ('that renowned one') is the subject, 'از جهان ناپدید' ('vanished from the world') is the predicate. The order places the verb before the nominal predicate, a usual poetic arrangement.
segments.s528-g55: 'چنین است' = 'such is' or 'thus is', followed by the noun 'کردار' ('deed/conduct') of the subject 'این چرخ پیر' ('this old Wheel'). 'چرخ پیر' is a poetic epithet for the Wheel of fortune/fate ('چرخ' here = fate/cycle), so the clause states a general, proverbial judgment about destiny.
segments.s528-g56: The paired 'چه ... و چه ...' is an idiomatic correlative meaning 'whether ... or ...'. Each 'با' means 'with' and names the two persons affected: 'اردوان' and 'اردشیر'. Thus the line generalizes that fate behaves the same 'with Ardavan or with Ardashir.'
segments.s528-g17: میان دو لشکر is a prepositional phrase ('between the two hosts') placed at the clause start for setting the location. دو پرتاب is a quantified noun phrase ('two throws/missiles') where the numeral precedes the noun; ماند (past of ماندن) is the intransitive verb 'remained/was left' and completes the clause—poetic inversion lets the location precede the subject.
segments.s528-g18: به خاک اندرون is an adverbial phrase of place ('into/within the earth'); اندرون is an archaic/literary word for 'inside'. مار بی‌تاب is the noun plus postpositive adjective ('the restless serpent') and ماند again is the past intransitive 'remained/lay'—so the clause reads literally 'into the inner earth the restless serpent remained.'
segments.s528-g19: خروشان سپاه is an adjective (active participle) before the noun ('the roaring/roaring host'); the coordinating و links to درفشان درفش, where درفشان is an adjective 'glittering/shining' modifying درفش ('the banner'). This is a parallel, emphatic pair of adjective+noun phrases common in epic poetry.
segments.s528-g20: سرافشان دل places the adjective 'srafāshaan' (here 'scattering, tossed about') before دل ('heart(s)') to mean 'hearts scattered/upturned.' از indicates cause ('because of/by') and governs تیغهای بنفش ('the purple swords/blades'); note the adjective بنفش follows تیغهای in poetic diction (the ezafe vowel is omitted in verse).
segments.s528-g21: چهل روز is a temporal phrase ('forty days'). زین سان is an archaic adverbial phrase meaning 'in this manner' (زین here is literary), and همی is a continuous/iterative marker ('continually/still'). جنگ بود uses بود (past of بودن) to state that 'the fighting was/continued'—overall: 'For forty days the war continued thus.'
segments.s528-g22: بران is a discourse pointer ('because of that' / 'thereon'), referring back to the previous clause. زیردستان جهان is a genitive-like grouping ('the subjects/vassals of the world'), and تنگ بود uses تنگ ('narrow/tight') with past بود to mean 'became narrow/strained'—i.e. life or the world grew constricted for the subjects.
segments.s528-g23: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/of' introducing source ('from every kind'). هرگونه‌ای means 'every kind/any sort' and تنگ شد is 'became scarce/tight' (تنگ used metaphorically); خوردنی ('eatables/food') is the noun that is said to have become scarce—so 'food of every kind became scarce.'
segments.s528-g24: همان here is anaphoric ('the same' → 'likewise'), followed by تنگ شد ('became narrow'). راه آوردنی is a nominal phrase where آوردنی is a deverbal adjective/gerund 'for bringing' modifying راه ('road/route'), so the clause says 'likewise the routes for bringing (supplies) became constricted.'
segments.s528-g97: برآورد is an archaic/past narrative verb here meaning 'brought forth' or 'arose' (third person past); زان is the fused prepositional phrase 'from that' (زان = ز + آن = 'from that/it'); چشمه is the noun 'spring' and is the source from which something arises; آتشکده is the compound noun 'fire-temple' and is the logical subject/result of برآورد. Word order is topical: 'from that spring' (زان چشمه) precedes the verb 'arose' (برآورد) and the created thing (آتشکده) follows, typical of older epic Persian.
segments.s528-g98: بدو is the prepositional-clitic phrase 'to it/unto it' (به + شو/ش), indicating that what follows pertains to the fire-temple; تازه شد is a two-word predicate where تازه (literally 'fresh/new') is predicated by the past auxiliary/verb شد ('became'/'was made'), giving 'was renewed' or 'became fresh'; مهر و جشن سده is a coordinated phrase where مهر (here the deity/cult of Mithra or the worship associated with Mithra) is joined by و to جشن سده ('the Sadeh festival'), the whole being the noun phrase that تازه شد modifies. Note the order: indirect object (to it) + predicate + theme.
segments.s528-g99: به گرد اندرش is a circumstantial prepositional phrase meaning 'around it/him' (به + گرد = 'around', اندر + ش = 'in/within + 3rd-person clitic'), placing the following list in space; باغ و میدان و کاخ is a simple coordination of nouns 'garden(s), square(s), and palace(ces)' that are the things located around the center. The possessive clitic ش on اندر marks the thing around which these were set.
segments.s528-g100: برآورده شد is a passive/resultative-style construction: برآورده (a verbal/adjectival form meaning 'brought about/provided/raised') plus شد ('became/was') yields 'was brought about/was raised'; جایگاه is the noun 'place' or 'seat' which is the thing formed; فراخ is an adjective 'spacious/wide' that modifies جایگاه. Word order places the verbal phrase before the noun it affects, a common epic ordering.
segments.s528-g101: چو شد is a temporal subordinating structure where چو = 'when' and شد is the past-tense copula/verb 'became/was'; شاه is the subject 'the king'; با introduces an instrumental/comitative phrase 'with' (با دانش و فر و زور) listing the qualities 'knowledge' (دانش), 'farr' (فر, the mythic royal glory), and 'strength' (زور). The preposition با governs the coordinated noun phrase that describes what the king possessed.
segments.s528-g102: همی is a narrative adverb often used to give a continuous or emphatic coloring ('then/so/indeed'); خواندش is خواند + object clitic ش and here means 'he called/named/appointed him' (خواندن = to call/name/summon in older Persian), with the clitic marking the person called; مرزبان شهر گور is the title and locality: مرزبان 'border-governor' plus شهر 'city' and گور (here a place-name, Gūr). The verb nominates the direct object as marzban of that city.
segments.s528-g103: به گرد اندرش is again the locative frame 'around it'; روستاها is the plural noun 'villages'; بساخت here functions as an older past-tense verb (from ساختن) meaning 'he built/constructed' (third person past). The sequence is typical SOV/auxiliary-order of classical Persian narrative: locative phrase + object (roosta‑ha) + verb (built).
segments.s528-g104: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; آباد is the adjective/participle 'prosperous/inhabited' and is the complement of کردش (کرد + clitic ش = 'he made/caused them'); کس is used in the negative sense 'no one' in this context; اندر introduces the subordinate phrase and نشاخت is an archaic verb meaning roughly 'rose up/contended' or 'made trouble' (here 'rebelled/contended'). The clause thus means that after he made them prosperous, no one opposed him. Note: several verbs/forms here are archaic and occur in Shahnameh narrative style.
segments.s528-g33: بترسید is a past/plural or perfect form meaning 'they feared' (here past narrative). زان is the old poetic contraction of از + آن, 'of/from that' (here 'of that army' = 'because/at the sight of that army'). لشکر اردوان is a genitive phrase 'the army of Ardāvān' (لشکر = 'army', اردوان = proper name). Word order is verb + prepositional phrase + noun phrase: 'they feared that army.'
segments.s528-g34: شدند is the past plural of شدن 'to become' ('they became'). اندرین = 'in this' or 'in this matter' (poetic locative), here marking the domain in which the following predicate holds. یک سخن is a nominal phrase 'one speech/one word' functioning with هم‌زبان as a predicate: هم‌زبان 'same‑tongued/like‑minded'. Together: 'they became, in this matter, of one tongue.'
segments.s528-g35: که introduces a causal/explanatory clause 'for/that'. این کار = 'this affair/act' (این = 'this', کار = 'affair/deed'). بر here has the sense 'upon/against/regarding' and links کار to اردوان ('with respect to Ardāvān'). ایزدیست is an adjectival/predicative compound meaning 'is divine / of God' (ایزدی + است). The clause says roughly 'for this affair is of God in regard to Ardāvān' (i.e. ordained by God concerning Ardāvān).
segments.s528-g36: بدین لشکر is a fused locative/causal phrase 'for/by this army / because of this army' (بدین = به + این ‘by/with this’). اکنون 'now' sets the time. بباید is the impersonal/modal present 'one must / it is necessary' (بباید + verb). گریست here is used as the lexical verb 'to weep/lament' (classical form); the construction بباید گریست = 'one must weep' (the verb appears in an older form).
segments.s528-g37: به روزی is a temporal phrase 'on a day / one day' (به here marking time). کجا in this poetic clause functions as a relative/temporal 'when' or 'where' introducing the circumstance. سخت شد = 'became severe' (سخت 'severe', شد past of شدن). کارزار = 'the battle/field' and is the subject of 'شد'. Together: 'on a day when the battle became severe.'
segments.s528-g38: همه 'everyone/all' is the subject. خواستند is past plural of خواستن 'they wished/they called out' (here best read as 'they cried/called for' together with the following utterance). آنگهی 'then/thereupon' gives the temporal relation. زینهار is an interjection 'Zinehâr!' meaning 'beware!' or 'take heed!' — the object/utterance of their calling. So: 'all cried then
segments.s528-g39: بیامد is the past form 'came' (narrative). ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. قلب سپاه اردشیر is a genitive chain 'the heart/center of Ardashir's army' (قلب used metaphorically as 'center'). The phrase means 'came from the centre of Ardashir's troops.'
segments.s528-g40: چکاچاک is an onomatopoetic or descriptive word meaning 'clinking/ glittering' (here describing the sound/sheen of weapons/armor) used adverbially/nominally. برخاست is past 'rose/was raised' (i.e. 'a clatter rose'). و = 'and' links the following image. باران تیر is a metaphorical noun phrase 'a rain (باران) of arrows (تیر)' meaning a heavy shower of arrows. Word order: descriptive noun/phrase + verb + and + figurative noun phrase.
segments.s528-g41: گرفتار شد is a compound/periphrastic past meaning “became trapped” or “was captured” (گرفتار = trapped + شد auxiliary). The locative phrase در میان (در + میان) follows the verb and means “in the midst/among,” and اردوان is the proper name that the phrase locates him among.
segments.s528-g42: بداد here functions as an epithet/noun ‘the Oppressor’ attached to the preceding name (poetic apposition). از پی is a frozen combination meaning ‘in pursuit of/after’ (از has a general relational sense and پی = 'following, pursuit'). تاج شیرین روان is a noun phrase: تاج 'crown' modified by the adjective شیرین 'sweet' and the word روان used poetically — together describing the coveted crown.
segments.s528-g43: به دست is a prepositional expression meaning ‘into the hand/power of’ (به = to/towards, دست used as ‘control/power’). یکی مرد is an idiomatic ‘a certain man’; خراد نام places خراد before نام to mean ‘named Kharad’ (نام = ‘name/so-called’).
segments.s528-g44: چو = ‘when’. بگرفت is the past of گرفتن ‘he seized’. بردش is بردن with the enclitic object -ش ‘him’ (here ‘he took/brought him away’). گرفته لگام is a participial phrase (گرفته = ‘having taken/seized’) with لگام ‘the bridle’ as its object: ‘having seized the bridle’ (a circumstantial participle modifying the action).
segments.s528-g45: به پیش = ‘before/in the presence of’ (literal ‘to the front of’). جهانجوی is an epithet meaning ‘world‑seeker’ (a title/epithet). بردش again is بردن + enclitic -ش ‘he brought/took him’, and اسیر is placed after the verb phrase as a predicate/object complement meaning ‘captive’.
segments.s528-g46: ز دور is an adverbial phrase ‘from afar’ (ز = from [poetic], دور = distant). اردوان را is the definite object marked by را. بدید is the simple past ‘saw’ and the subject اردشیر follows the verb for poetical word order/emphasis (‘Ardashir saw Ardavan’ with inverted word order).
segments.s528-g47: فرود آمد is a past-tense verbal phrase meaning ‘alighted/descended/dismounted’ (فرود = descent/coming down + auxiliary آمد). از باره here uses از + باره (‘from the load/charge/burden’), read poetically as ‘from the charge/pressure’ or ‘from his burden/horse’; شاه اردوان is the title + name and functions as the subject following the verb.
segments.s528-g48: تنش is تن + enclitic ش ‘his body’. خسته is a predicate/adjectival past participle ‘weary/tired’. تیر with و تیره‌روان is a coordinated phrase: ‘weary from arrows (تیر) and (also) ill‑fated/gloomy (تیره‌روان)’ — two reasons/qualities joined by و.
segments.s528-g81: This is a two-part imperative/advice addressed to 'تو' (you). 'تو' is the 2nd-person pronoun; 'فرمان‌بر' is a predicative noun/adjective meaning 'obedient' with an elided copula (i.e. 'be obedient'). The conjunction 'و' links that clause to the verb phrase 'دختر او بخواه' where 'بخواه' is an imperative/subjunctive 'ask/seek' (2nd‑person singular) and 'دختر او' is its direct object ('his daughter').
segments.s528-g82: 'که' introduces a subordinate/result clause ('so that' / 'that'), linking this clause to what follows. 'با فر' = 'with (the) far/royal glory' (an instrumental prepositional phrase). 'برزست' is an archaic/poetic third‑person form meaning 'is exalted / rises high' (descriptive of the implied beneficiary) and functions predicatively. The second 'با' again marks accompaniment ('with crown and ...'); 'تاج و گاه' is a paired phrase 'crown and seat/throne' (classical collocation).
segments.s528-g83: The verbal phrase 'به دست آیدت' uses the idiom 'به دست آمدن' (to come into the hand) with the enclitic 'ـت' marking the indirect object 'to you' — here meaning 'you will obtain' or 'will come into your possession'. The following nouns 'افسر و تاج و گنج' are a coordinated list of objects (helmet, crown, treasure).
segments.s528-g84: 'کجا' is an interrogative/adverb 'where' (here rhetorical), 'اردوان' is the proper name (Ardavan), and 'گرد کرد' is a compound verbal idea: 'گرد کردن' (to collect/bring together) with 'کرد' as the past tense auxiliary 'did/made' (past 3rd sg). 'آن' is a demonstrative pronoun 'those/that' referring back to the treasures, and 'به رنج' is an adverbial phrase 'with hardship / through toil' qualifying how Ardavan gathered them.
segments.s528-g85: 'ازو' = 'from him' (prepositional/ablative clitic), 'پند' = 'advice/counsel' is the object of 'بشنید' (past tense 3rd‑person 'he heard'). The conjunction 'و' links to 'گفتا' (past 'he said'). 'رواست' is a fused copular statement: 'روا' (fitting/permissible) + 'ست' (is) = 'it is fitting/allowed.'
segments.s528-g86: 'هم' here means 'also/at once' or 'immediately'; 'اندر زمان' is a temporal prepositional phrase 'in that time / at that moment.' 'دختر او بخواست' is a past action: 'he asked/requested his daughter' ('بخواست' = past 3rd‑person of خواستن meaning 'he sought/asked for'). The whole segment states that he promptly made the request.
segments.s528-g87: 'به ایوان او' is a locative prepositional phrase 'at/to his iwan (audience‑hall/porch)'. There is no explicit verb in this short clause; the duration 'یک دو ماه' ('one or two months') together with the archaic particle 'همی' (a durative/continuative marker often used in epic diction) indicates a period of staying or presence (the verb 'to stay' is implied/omitted, a common epic ellipsis). 'بد' here appears as an archaic/adverbial element in the phrase and is best read as part of the elliptical locative/durative construction rather than a full descriptive adjective.
segments.s528-g88: This is a pair of nominal epithets in apposition. 'توانگر' (an epithet meaning 'wealthy' or 'powerful') modifies 'سپهبد' (spahbed, a military commander) and is then repeated to modify 'سپاه' (army/host). There is no verb here; the line lists the notable figures/forces present or described.
segments.s528-g1: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when' introducing the clause. آگاهی آمد is a simple verb phrase 'news/notice came' with آگاهی as the subject/thing that arrives. سوی اردوان is a prepositional phrase 'to Ardavan' (سوی = toward/to) specifying the recipient; word order is verb-final for the clause's action plus the destination after it.
segments.s528-g2: دلش is دل (heart) plus the enclitic possessive -ش 'his', so 'his heart' is the logical subject. گشت is the past intransitive 'became/turned'. پربیم is a compound adjective 'full of fear' (پر + بیم), and و links the second adjective تیره‌روان 'gloomy/dark‑souled'. The sequence Subject + گشت + predicative adjectives expresses 'his heart became X and Y'.
segments.s528-g3: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' introducing reported speech. گفت here functions as the verb 'said' (note: the provided sense list doesn't show 'said' so that token is treated as a verb in the explanation). کین is an archaic/dialectal demonstrative meaning 'this' (not the common sense 'vengeance'), introducing the noun phrase راز چرخ بلند 'the secret of the lofty sky/heaven' where چرخ بلند is a genitival phrase 'lofty heaven/sky' modifying راز.
segments.s528-g4: همی before a verb marks continuous or habitual action ('kept saying' / 'continually'). گفت again functions as 'said' (not matched in the supplied sense glosses). با من is the prepositional phrase 'to/with me' (با = with/to, من = me). خداوند پند is a subject+object pairing 'the Lord (خداوند) [gave] counsel (پند)' where word order is somewhat inverted in the poetic phrasing; overall: 'the Lord continually gave me counsel.'
segments.s528-g5: هران is the fused/poetic form (هر آن) meaning 'that one' / 'he who' (i.e. 'whoever'), followed by بد 'bad/evil' forming a predication 'evil is he who...'. کز is a relative particle 'who/that/from which' introducing the relative clause. اندیشه بیرون بود literally 'was outside of thought' = 'was without thought' where بود is the past copula 'was'.
segments.s528-g6: ز marks 'from' (poetic register). بخشش here means 'giving/donation/generosity'. به marks the destination 'to/into' and کوشش is 'effort/striving'. گذر is a noun 'passage/passing' and is questioned by چون بود 'how was' (چون = how; بود = was), so the whole clause asks about the nature/quality of the passage 'from giving to effort'. Word order places the questioned element before the copula (چون بود).
segments.s528-g7: گمانی نبردم is an idiomatic negative past construction 'I did not suppose / I could not have imagined' where گمانی is 'a supposition' and نبردم (lit. 'I did not take') functions with it to mean 'I did not think/suppose'. که از اردشیر introduces the content of that negative supposition: 'that from Ardashir...'; از marks origin 'from'.
segments.s528-g8: یکی here means 'a certain / someone' (indefinite subject). نامجوی is a compound meaning 'seeker of a name' (i.e. fame-seeker / one seeking renown). آید is the verb 'comes' (present/habitual or simple narrative present). و links the coordinated predicate/appositive شهرگیر 'city‑conqueror' (a besieger). The phrase says that such a person would come from Ardashir.
segments.s528-g73: خروشان is an adjectival/adverbial participle 'roaring' that modifies the action that follows; بشستش is past tense of شستن with the enclitic object -ش 'him/it' (he washed him/it). ز is the ablative preposition 'from', خاک here means 'dust' (the residue of battle), and نبرد is a genitive complement 'of battle' (خاکِ نبرد = 'the dust of battle'). Word order places the prepositional phrase z + noun + modifier after the verb in poetic style.
segments.s528-g74: بر آیین شاهان is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in/according to the ritual/custom of the kings' (بر used here as 'in accordance with/on the basis of'). یکی دخمه کرد is a simple clause with یکی 'one/a single' modifying دخمه 'tomb' and کرد (past of کردن) as the main verb 'made/did' — typical SOV/verb-final narrative order in classical Persian.
segments.s528-g75: به دیبا is an adverbial/instrumental phrase 'in/with brocade' placed before the verb; بپوشید is past tense of پوشیدن 'he/they clothed/covered'. خسته is an adjectival noun 'the weary/worn one' used here as the (dead) body being dressed. برش in this line functions as a fused form with the pronominal clitic -ش ('on/for him' or 'his'), but the token's dictionary senses ('cut/slice') don't apply here, so it should be read as a clitic pronoun attached to the preceding element.
segments.s528-g76: ز کافور is a material-phrase 'of/from camphor' (indicating the substance used); کرد is the main verb 'made'; افسری is the object 'a diadem/ornament for the head'; بر سرش is a prepositional phrase 'upon his head' where سر 'head' takes the possessive clitic ش 'his'. The clause follows canonical object-before-verb order and places the locative/possessive phrase after the noun.
segments.s528-g77: به پیمود uses پیمود (past of پیمودن) 'he traversed/covered'; the initial به here is a pro‑locutional preposition often used with motion verbs. آن خاک کاخش is a noun phrase 'that earth/ground of his palace' (کاخ with possessive clitic -ش). به پی is a phrase meaning 'in pursuit of' (پی 'pursuit'), so the clause reads 'he traversed the palace ground in pursuit (of...)' — the verb governs the following pursuit phrase.
segments.s528-g78: ز لشکر is an ablative 'from the army/host'; هران‌کس is an indefinite relative 'whoever/anyone'; که introduces a relative clause; شد is past tense 'became/turned/went' used here intransitively to mean 'went/turned (in that direction)'; سوی ری is the directional phrase 'toward Rey'. The whole clause specifies 'whoever of the army went toward Rey.'
segments.s528-g79: وزان پس is a compound adverbial 'and/from that, then' (وزان = 'from that'; پس = 'then/afterwards'), placed before the verb for temporal sequence. بیامد is past tense 'he came', and بر اردشیر is the prepositional phrase 'to/onto Ardashir' (بر here marking direction toward the named person). The line follows typical narrative order: temporal adverbs → verb → prepositional complement.
segments.s528-g80: چنین گفت is a formulaic phrase 'thus he said' (چنین 'thus/so' + گفت past of گفتن 'said'). کای is an archaic vocative particle 'O' used when addressing kings; شاه is the vocative 'king'; دانش‌پذیر is an epithet 'receptive/teachable of knowledge' (used here as an epithet/compliment addressed to the king). Note: the lemma senses supplied for گفت in the list were nominal ('conversation, dialogue') and do not match the verbal use here, so the sense index for گفت is left unspecified.
segments.s528-g9: در گنج بگشاد is literal word order: preposition/nominal phrase + verb. در here is the noun 'door/gate' idiomatically 'the door of the treasury' and گنج is the treasury/vault; بگشاد is the past form (archaic/poetic) with the prefix بـ + گشادن meaning 'opened'. و links the following clause; روزی is the noun 'sustenance/provisions' (here the thing given) and بداد is the past of دادن in the same poetic prefixed form (بـ + داد) meaning 'gave'.
segments.s528-g10: سپه is the noun 'army/host'. بر گرفت is a compound verbal phrase: the prefix بر with گرفت (past of گرفتن) gives the sense 'took up/raised' (he mustered or took the host up). و coordinates the next clause; بنه is the camp/encampment (object) and برنهاد is the past of نهادن with the same poetic prefix meaning 'set up/erected'. Word order places the object (بنه) immediately before its verb (برنهاد), a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s528-g11: ز … ز is the poetic preposition 'from' repeated for two place-names (emphatic listing). گیل and دیلم name regions/peoples (Gilan, Daylam); بیامد is the past form of آمدن (here 'came') using the verb with the prefix بی- (a poetic/paragogic form), and سپاه is the noun 'host/army' which functions as the logical subject of بیامد though placed after it — V–(postposed) subject order is common in verse.
segments.s528-g12: همی is an archaic/epic adverb meaning 'meanwhile' or marking continuous action. گرد here functions as 'dust/whirl' (the rising dust of the troops) rather than the preposition 'around'. لشکر names the army; برآمد is a past verb 'arose/issued forth' (dust or commotion rose). به ماه gives the temporal/scene-setting phrase 'under/with the moon' or 'at moonrise' (به + ماه marking relation to the moon).
segments.s528-g13: وزان روی is a compact poetic locution: وزان = و + زان 'and/from that', meaning 'from that time/for that reason'; روی here is part of the idiom (literally 'face' but used adverbially). لشکر بیاورد شاه is inverted word order: لشکر (object/army) then بیاورد (past of آوردن 'brought') then شاه (the king, subject); the verb is in the past (indicative) with the poetic prefix بی-/آ‑ form and the subject follows the verb for stylistic effect.
segments.s528-g14: سپاهی here begins a relative clause: که is the relative pronoun 'that/which'. بر باد is a short prepositional/phrasing element with باد 'wind' as its noun; بربست is past of بربستن ('to bind/close/seal') with the prefixed بــ, so the verb here means 'shut/closed/blocked'. راه is the object 'way/road'. Grammatically the clause describes the army (سپاهی) as the one 'which closed/blocked the road'.
segments.s528-g15: ز بس is an idiomatic prepositional phrase 'from so much/so many'; ناله بوق is a genitive construction 'the cry/clamor of the horn' (ناله + instrument); و با introduces another instrument phrase 'and with'; کرنای is the oblique form of کرنا 'trumpet' and stands in the same instrumental/genitive relation. The sequence lists sounds produced by instruments.
segments.s528-g16: ترنگیدن is a verbal noun/infinitival form meaning 'the ringing/sounding' (the lemma as given appears slightly irregular/archaic in the file, but here it functions as the action noun). زنگ is 'bell/ringing'; و coordinates; هندی modifies the following noun as an adjective 'Indian'; درای is an archaic/poetic noun (here 'drums' or an instrument). The whole phrase lists additional sounds — bells and Indian drums — produced along with the horns and trumpets.

sentences.s528-s45: Namjuy came from Ray toward Pars, relieved of war and of dispute.
sentences.s528-s46: He made a certain town full of palaces and gardens; within it were springs and plains and longing.
sentences.s528-s47: Which (place) is now called by the venerable old dehqan, they call him 'Khore Ardashir'.
sentences.s528-s48: A certain boundless spring within — abundantly from it a river opened and a stream.
sentences.s528-s13: So many were slain on the Hamun plain that the piled bodies rose like a mountain; (he) grew exhausted and sick of life.
sentences.s528-s14: At last a dark cloud arose; the machinery of effort and battle was put in place.
sentences.s528-s15: A wind rose from the assembly; the warriors' hearts became, because of that, broken/terrified.
sentences.s528-s16: The mountain spat and the plain trembled; its roar still passed through the air.
sentences.s528-s53: At a certain place he saw a deep sea; then a mountain rose up and came before him.
sentences.s528-s54: They carried (it) off — the workmen and the men of deed — and from that mountain they cut a hundred streams.
sentences.s528-s55: He (or they) drove (it/them) from the mountain as far as the city of graves; that town became full of houses and cattle.
sentences.s528-s29: If even a star should rise high, at last it will consign (him) to the base earth.
sentences.s528-s30: His two children were also taken; then the offspring of Arash became despised.
sentences.s528-s31: He had those two bound foot and hand in fetters, and the lofty king sent them to prison.
sentences.s528-s32: The two ill‑disposed fled from the battle, and they did not fall into the snare of calamity.
sentences.s528-s33: They went away weeping to India; if you deem it fitting, make a tale from these words.
sentences.s528-s34: The whole battlefield was full of praise, and the girdles (waists) were full of arms, troops, silver and gold.
sentences.s528-s35: He commanded that they assemble; then the king bestowed everything upon the army.
sentences.s528-s36: He withdrew from among the great smiths; he wiped the blood from Ardavan's body.
sentences.s528-s25: King Ardashir ordered the torturer to seize the enemy king.
sentences.s528-s26: With a dagger, split his middle into two halves; fill the hearts of the ill-omened with fear.
sentences.s528-s27: The fortress-warden came and carried out the order; that renowned man disappeared from the world.
sentences.s528-s28: Such is the action of this aged Wheel of fate — whether with Ardavan or with Ardashir.
sentences.s528-s9: Between the two hosts two missiles remained suspended; the restless serpent lay buried within the earth.
sentences.s528-s10: The host roared and the banner flashed; hearts were scattered by the purple blades.
sentences.s528-s11: The battle continued like this for forty days; because of that the subjects of the world found the world narrow.
sentences.s528-s12: Every sort of food grew scarce; likewise the roads for bringing (supplies) became narrow.
sentences.s528-s49: From that spring a fire-temple arose; to it the worship of Mehr and the Sadeh festival were renewed.
sentences.s528-s50: Around it gardens, squares, and palaces were raised; a wide place was formed.
sentences.s528-s51: When the king was endowed with knowledge, farr (royal glory) and might, he called him marzban of the city of Gūr.
sentences.s528-s52: He built many villages around it; when he made them prosperous, no one rose against him.
sentences.s528-s17: They feared that army of Ardāvān; in this matter they became of one tongue.
sentences.s528-s18: For this affair is of God concerning Ardāvān; for this army one must now lament.
sentences.s528-s19: On a day when the battle became severe, everyone then cried, 'Beware!'
sentences.s528-s20: From the heart of Ardashir's host came a glittering clatter, and a rain of arrows arose.
sentences.s528-s21: He was taken (became trapped) in the midst of Ardavan the Oppressor, in pursuit of the sweet crown.
sentences.s528-s22: He fell into the hands of a certain man named Kharad; when he seized him he led him off, having taken the bridle.
sentences.s528-s23: He brought him captive before the world‑seeker; from afar Ardashir saw Ardavan.
sentences.s528-s24: King Ardavan dismounted (fell down); his body was weary from arrows and ill‑fated.
sentences.s528-s41: Be obedient and ask for his daughter, so that by (her) farr and exaltation and with crown and throne.
sentences.s528-s42: Helmet and crown and treasure will come into your hand; where did Ardavan gather those with such toil?
sentences.s528-s43: He heard counsel from him and said, 'It is fitting.' At once, in that same moment, he requested his daughter.
sentences.s528-s44: They remained at his iwan for a month or two — the wealthy spahbed, the wealthy host/army.
sentences.s528-s1: When the news reached Ardavan, his heart became full of fear and grew gloomy.
sentences.s528-s2: Thus spoke the lofty secret of the heavens: 'The Lord continually gave me counsel.'
sentences.s528-s3: Evil is he who is without thought; how (then) was the passage from giving to effort?
sentences.s528-s4: I did not suppose that from Ardashir there would come a certain man seeking renown and a conqueror of cities.
sentences.s528-s37: The roaring (waters) washed the battle-dust from him, and according to the kings' custom they made a single tomb.
sentences.s528-s38: They clothed the weary one in brocade, and of camphor they made a diadem upon his head.
sentences.s528-s39: He crossed/traversed that earth of his palace in pursuit of whoever from the army went toward Rey.
sentences.s528-s40: From that (time) he then came to Ardashir and said thus, 'O king, receptive of learning...'
sentences.s528-s5: He opened the treasury and gave out provisions; he raised the host and set up the camp.
sentences.s528-s6: From Gilan and from Daylam a host came; meanwhile the army's dust rose and the host advanced beneath the moon.
sentences.s528-s7: From that moment the king brought an army — warriors who had shut the way.
sentences.s528-s8: With so many cries of horn and the blare of trumpets, the ringing of bells and Indian drums was heard.
